markdown:
The Supply Curve showcases the positive relationship referred to by the **law of supply **(2.2.1) in a graph. Hence, higher prices lead to more quantity supplied, _ceteris paribus_.

![Figure 1: the supply curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3fb9507071cf8fdac9e54774c45b1159cbc3b890-2338x1813.png)

The figure above showcases a **supply curve ($S$)** that is **upward-sloping** due to the **law of supply**:

1. For a price of $2$, the firm is **willing and able** to supply an output of $20$ units
2. As the price **rises** to $3,4$ and $5$, the quantity supplied by the firm **also rises** to $30, 40$ and $50$ respectively.

<callout type="tip">#### Drawing the supply curve


Three **important** points to make sure of when drawing the supply diagram.

1. Make sure to **label the axis** (holds for any diagram). In this case, prices $P$ will be on the $y$-axis (add the currency being used too) and quantity $Q$ will be on the $x$-axis
2. The graph should show an **upward-sloping **curve (positive relationship) such that a rise in price leads to a rise in the quantity supplied
3. If you're showcasing a specific point on the curve, make sure to **label the price and quantity** associated with it clearly. For example label the price $P_1$ and quantity as $Q_{s1}$</callout>

<callout type="note">1. It is **important **to note that the supply curve **_doesn't _**indicate anything about the **actual** quantity the firm will supply and the price it will receive.
2. The curve indicates what the firm is** ready to** and can **potentially** supply in the market.</callout>

### The vertical supply curve


In some scenarios, the supply curve may be vertical. A **vertical supply curve** indicates that:

> Even when prices change, the quantity supplied **stays the same** as seen in the figure below.

![Figure 2: vertical supply curve illustrating fixed supply](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ead388f445fac2584c9444f00366b759d89eaeb9-2338x1813.png)

The **vertical supply** **curve** occurs** **when there is:

1. **Limited time** and therefore it is **not possible** to produce more of a certain good or service within that period. For example seats at a cinema cannot easily be added quickly, their supply is fixed (the number of seats in the cinema).
2. **No possibility of new production** of that good or service ever. For example, original paintings and antiques can only be replicated but not reproduced.

These two conditions lead to the supply curve being fixed at a given quantity despite the changes in price.

### Marginal Cost Curve and Supply Curve (HL Only)


![Figure 3: the supply curve as the upward-slopping section of the marginal cost curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ad11256e38007a8a91f57d696b5fe0377a49eb54-2338x1813.png)

The above figure shows a **marginal cost curve** representing the cost required to produce one more unit of the good or service.

The supply curve showcases all the quantities that a firm is **able and willing** to produce for a given price.

Hence, **a section** of the **marginal cost curve** is the firm's **supply curve**. (_The exact part of the marginal cost curve will be discussed in 2.11_)

* Because the firm is only **willing** to produce **one more unit** of the good if it can **cover** the marginal cost
* Hence, the **marginal cost** **curve** tells us the cost needed for the firm to be **able **to produce **one more unit**
* It also showcases the **minimum price** the firm will charge for it to be **willing** to produce **one more unit** as it needs to cover the marginal costs

The **section** of the marginal cost curve where the firm **would not** benefit from **shutting down** is the supply curve.

<callout type="tok">Consider goods or services with limited supply, like rare artwork or land. How do these exceptions challenge the universality of the law of supply?</callout>

<callout type="self_review">Can you explain why the supply curve slopes upward? Try drawing a supply curve and annotating it with reasons for the positive relationship between price and quantity supplied.</callout>
